name: Laughing Kookaburra
funFact: Their dawn 'laughing' chorus marks territory boundaries.

name: Australian Magpie
funFact: Magpies can recognise up to 100 individual human faces.

name: Rainbow Lorikeet
funFact: Their brush-tipped tongues are specially adapted for nectar.

name: Galah
funFact: 'Galah' in Aussie slang means acting foolishly — from their playful antics.

name: Willie Wagtail
funFact: Rides on the backs of cattle to catch disturbed insects.

name: Superb Fairy-wren
funFact: Males pluck yellow petals as courtship gifts.

name: Australian Pelican
funFact: Their bill pouch can hold up to 13 litres of water.

name: Emu
funFact: Emus can sprint at 50 km/h and are excellent swimmers.

name: Wedge-tailed Eagle
funFact: Soars at altitudes over 2,000 metres with a 2.3m wingspan.

name: Sulphur-crested Cockatoo
funFact: Can live over 80 years and teaches others to open bins.

name: Noisy Miner
funFact: Forms aggressive colonies that drive out smaller birds.

name: Australian White Ibis
funFact: Nicknamed 'bin chicken' for its urban bin-raiding habits.

name: Silvereye
funFact: Migrates in large flocks along the east coast each autumn.

name: Crimson Rosella
funFact: Young birds are olive-green for their first two years.

name: Eastern Rosella
funFact: One of the most brightly-coloured parrots in the world.

name: Pied Butcherbird
funFact: Considered one of Australia's finest songbirds — melodic flute-like calls.

name: Grey Butcherbird
funFact: Wedges prey in tree forks to 'butcher' it — hence the name.

name: Australian Raven
funFact: Their slow, drawn-out 'aah-aah-aaaah' call is unmistakable.

name: Pied Currawong
funFact: Famous for their loud 'curra-wong' call echoing through forests.

name: Welcome Swallow
funFact: Named for their arrival at the start of spring.

name: Masked Lapwing
funFact: Known for dive-bombing anyone who ventures too near their ground nests.

name: Straw-necked Ibis
funFact: Nicknamed 'farmer's friend' for eating swarming locusts.

name: Royal Spoonbill
funFact: Sweeps its spoon-shaped bill side-to-side through water to feed.

name: Australian Wood Duck
funFact: One of the few ducks that nests in tree hollows.

name: Pacific Black Duck
funFact: Hybridises freely with introduced Mallards, threatening pure populations.

name: Chestnut Teal
funFact: Males are iridescent green-headed; females plain speckled brown.

name: Grey Teal
funFact: Nomadic — follows rains across the continent to ephemeral lakes.

name: Black Swan
funFact: The floral emblem of Western Australia.

name: Little Pied Cormorant
funFact: Dives underwater and swims using webbed feet to chase fish.

name: Great Cormorant
funFact: Its feathers are not fully waterproof — often seen drying wings outstretched.

name: Australasian Darter
funFact: Swims with only its snake-like neck above water — hence 'snakebird'.

name: White-faced Heron
funFact: The most commonly seen heron in Australia.

name: Cattle Egret
funFact: Self-introduced to Australia in the 1940s from Asia.

name: Tawny Frogmouth
funFact: Often mistaken for owls — they're actually related to nightjars.

name: Crested Pigeon
funFact: Wings produce a whistling sound in flight — a built-in alarm.

name: Spotted Dove
funFact: Introduced in the 1800s; now common across eastern cities.

name: Common Bronzewing
funFact: Iridescent bronze wing-patches flash in sunlight.

name: Rainbow Bee-eater
funFact: Whacks stinging insects against a branch to remove the stinger before eating.

name: Sacred Kingfisher
funFact: Revered in Polynesian mythology as having control over the seas.

name: Blue-winged Kookaburra
funFact: Has an eerie cackling call very different from its laughing cousin.

name: Red Wattlebird
funFact: Named for the bright red fleshy wattles beside its eyes.

name: Little Wattlebird
funFact: Despite its name, actually lacks wattles entirely.

name: Noisy Friarbird
funFact: Named for its bald black head, said to resemble a friar's tonsure.

name: New Holland Honeyeater
funFact: One of the first Australian birds described by European scientists.

name: White-plumed Honeyeater
funFact: The distinctive white neck plume lights up like a paint stroke.

name: Eastern Spinebill
funFact: One of the few Australian birds that hovers like a hummingbird.

name: Satin Bowerbird
funFact: Males collect blue objects — pegs, straws, bottle caps — to decorate bowers.

name: Regent Bowerbird
funFact: Males are black with brilliant gold head and wing bands.

name: Great Bowerbird
funFact: Builds the largest bower of any species — up to a metre tall.

name: Australian Brush-turkey
funFact: Males build huge compost-mound nests and control the temperature with their beak.

name: Magpie-lark
funFact: Not a true magpie or lark — builds a unique mud-cup nest.

name: Grey Fantail
funFact: Constantly fans its tail to flush insects from foliage.

name: Rufous Fantail
funFact: Migrates thousands of kilometres between summer and winter ranges.

name: Golden Whistler
funFact: Males have one of the most beautiful whistled songs in Australia.

name: Rufous Whistler
funFact: Sings its rich 'ee-chong' call throughout the day, even in summer heat.

name: Grey Shrike-thrush
funFact: Considered one of Australia's most accomplished songsters.

name: White-browed Scrubwren
funFact: A tiny bird that lurks in dense undergrowth with harsh scolding calls.

name: Brown Thornbill
funFact: Can mimic the alarm calls of larger birds to scare off predators.

name: Splendid Fairy-wren
funFact: Males turn deep cobalt blue only during the breeding season.

name: Variegated Fairy-wren
funFact: The most widespread fairy-wren — found across most of the continent.

name: Red-backed Fairy-wren
funFact: Breeding males are jet black with a flame-red saddle.

name: Australian King-Parrot
funFact: Males have scarlet heads; females have all-green heads.

name: Eastern Koel
funFact: A brood parasite — lays eggs in other birds' nests.

name: Black-faced Cuckoo-shrike
funFact: After landing, shuffles its wings in a distinctive 'rearranging' motion.

name: Eastern Yellow Robin
funFact: Perches sideways on tree trunks, waiting to pounce on prey below.

name: Jacky Winter
funFact: Sings its namesake 'jacky-jacky-jacky' on cold winter mornings.

name: Superb Lyrebird
funFact: Can mimic chainsaws, camera shutters, and car alarms with uncanny accuracy.

name: Budgerigar
funFact: Forms vast nomadic flocks of thousands across the inland.

name: Cockatiel
funFact: The smallest of Australia's cockatoos.

name: Nankeen Kestrel
funFact: Hovers motionless in mid-air while scanning for prey below.

name: Peregrine Falcon
funFact: The fastest animal on Earth — dives at over 300 km/h.

name: Brown Falcon
funFact: Often hunts at grass fires, snatching escaping prey.

name: Whistling Kite
funFact: Named for its loud whistling call given in flight.

name: Black Kite
funFact: Known to pick up burning sticks to spread fires and flush prey.

name: Silver Gull
funFact: Australia's most familiar gull — the classic beach 'seagull'.

name: Azure Kingfisher
funFact: A jewel-bright bird the size of a sparrow, rarely seen perched.

name: Forest Kingfisher
funFact: Perches prominently then plunges to the ground for prey.

name: Dusky Moorhen
funFact: Cooperatively breeds — multiple adults help raise a single brood.

name: Australasian Swamphen
funFact: Grasps food with one giant red foot while feeding — like a hand.

name: Eurasian Coot
funFact: Its toes are lobed rather than webbed — a unique adaptation for swimming.

name: Red-capped Plover
funFact: Fakes a broken wing to lure predators away from its nest.

name: Australasian Grebe
funFact: Carries chicks on its back while swimming between waterways.

name: Striated Thornbill
funFact: Frequently joins mixed-species feeding flocks in winter.

name: Major Mitchell's Cockatoo
funFact: Mates for life and can live over 75 years in captivity.

name: Red-tailed Black Cockatoo
funFact: Males have brilliant red tail panels visible only in flight.

name: Yellow-tailed Black Cockatoo
funFact: Rips apart banksia cones and wood with enormous force.

name: Glossy Black Cockatoo
funFact: Feeds almost exclusively on casuarina cones — critically habitat-dependent.

name: Bush Stone-curlew
funFact: Unnerving night-time wailing calls have earned it ghostly folklore.

name: Brolga
funFact: Famous for its elegant leaping courtship dance.

name: Plumed Whistling Duck
funFact: Forms nomadic flocks of thousands in the tropical north.

name: Musk Duck
funFact: Males have a fleshy black lobe under the bill and a musky odour.

name: Barking Owl
funFact: Its eerie 'screaming-woman' call has terrified bush travellers for centuries.

name: Australian Masked Owl
funFact: Tasmanian subspecies is the world's largest Tyto owl.

name: Hooded Plover
funFact: Nests on sand above the high-tide line — extremely vulnerable to disturbance.

name: Rockwarbler
funFact: New South Wales' only endemic bird — restricted to sandstone country.

name: Palm Cockatoo
funFact: The only birds known to use tools as musical instruments, drumming sticks on trees.

name: Powerful Owl
funFact: Carries prey back to a favourite perch and holds it all day.

name: Australian Bustard
funFact: Males perform booming courtship displays, inflating throat sacs.

name: Gang-gang Cockatoo
funFact: Makes a creaking sound like a rusty hinge when flying.

name: Superb Parrot
funFact: Restricted to river-red-gum woodlands of the Murray-Murrumbidgee.

name: Malleefowl
funFact: Male tends a compost nest-mound year-round, keeping it at a constant 33°C.

name: Grey Falcon
funFact: One of Australia's rarest birds of prey — possibly fewer than 1,000 pairs.

name: Princess Parrot
funFact: Named after Princess Alexandra — an elusive nomad of the Western Deserts.

name: Black-breasted Buzzard
funFact: Drops stones on emu eggs to crack them open — rare tool use in raptors.

name: Letter-winged Kite
funFact: Unique among raptors for being strictly nocturnal.

name: Southern Cassowary
funFact: Considered the most dangerous bird — kicks with dagger-like claws.

name: Red Goshawk
funFact: One of the world's rarest raptors — only a few hundred pairs remain.

name: Helmeted Honeyeater
funFact: Victoria's faunal emblem — fewer than 250 wild birds remain.

name: Eastern Bristlebird
funFact: Prefers running through undergrowth to flying — critically fire-sensitive.

name: Mallee Emu-wren
funFact: A feather-weight 4-gram bird — one of Australia's smallest.

name: Black-eared Miner
funFact: Hybridises with Yellow-throated Miners — threatening genetic identity.

name: Golden-shouldered Parrot
funFact: Nests only in termite mounds on Cape York savannah.

name: Regent Honeyeater
funFact: So rare, young males have forgotten their song and mimic other species.

name: Night Parrot
funFact: Presumed extinct for a century — photographed alive only in 2013.

name: Orange-bellied Parrot
funFact: One of only three migratory parrot species — fewer than 70 wild adults.

name: Swift Parrot
funFact: Flies the Bass Strait each year — predated heavily by sugar gliders.

name: Plains-wanderer
funFact: A living fossil — the sole member of its ancient family.

name: Western Ground Parrot
funFact: Only around 150 remain in a single Western Australian reserve.

name: Australasian Bittern
funFact: Males produce a deep 'booming' call that travels kilometres at dusk.
